From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

British comedian and actor (born 1962)

| Eddie Izzard | |
| --- | --- |
| Izzard in 2015 | |

| Other names | Suzy Eddie Izzard |

**Suzy Eddie Izzard[[a]](#cite_note-3)** ([/ˈɪzɑːrd/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*IZ-ard*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); born **Edward John Izzard**, 7 February 1962) is a British stand-up comedian, actor and activist. Her[[b]](#cite_note-6) comedic style takes the form of what appears to the audience as rambling whimsical monologues.

Izzard's stand-up comedy tours have included *[Live at the Ambassadors](/wiki/Live_at_the_Ambassadors "Live at the Ambassadors")* (1993), *[Definite Article](/wiki/Definite_Article "Definite Article")* (1996), *[Glorious](/wiki/Glorious_(Eddie_Izzard) "Glorious (Eddie Izzard)")* (1997), *[Dress to Kill](/wiki/Dress_to_Kill_(Eddie_Izzard) "Dress to Kill (Eddie Izzard)")* (1998), *[Circle](/wiki/Circle_(Eddie_Izzard) "Circle (Eddie Izzard)")* (2000), *[Stripped](/wiki/Stripped_(tour) "Stripped (tour)")* (2009), *[Force Majeure](/wiki/Force_Majeure_(tour) "Force Majeure (tour)")* (2013) and *Wunderbar* (2022). She starred in the television series *[The Riches](/wiki/The_Riches "The Riches")* (2007–2008) and has appeared in numerous films, including *[Ocean's Twelve](/wiki/Ocean%27s_Twelve "Ocean's Twelve")* (2004), *[Ocean's Thirteen](/wiki/Ocean%27s_Thirteen "Ocean's Thirteen")* (2007), *[Valkyrie](/wiki/Valkyrie_(film) "Valkyrie (film)")* (2008), *[Absolutely Anything](/wiki/Absolutely_Anything "Absolutely Anything")* (2015) and *[Six Minutes to Midnight](/wiki/Six_Minutes_to_Midnight "Six Minutes to Midnight")* (2020). Izzard has also worked as a voice actor on films such as *[Five Children and It](/wiki/Five_Children_and_It_(2004_film) "Five Children and It (2004 film)")* (2004), *[The Wild](/wiki/The_Wild "The Wild")* (2006), *[The Lego Batman Movie](/wiki/The_Lego_Batman_Movie "The Lego Batman Movie")* (2017) and the [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") original series *[Green Eggs and Ham](/wiki/Green_Eggs_and_Ham_(TV_series) "Green Eggs and Ham (TV series)")* (2019). Among various accolades, she won two [Primetime Emmys](/wiki/Primetime_Emmys "Primetime Emmys") for *Dress to Kill* and was nominated for a [Tony Award](/wiki/Tony_Award "Tony Award") for her Broadway performance in *[A Day in the Death of Joe Egg](/wiki/A_Day_in_the_Death_of_Joe_Egg "A Day in the Death of Joe Egg")*.

In 2009, Izzard completed 43 marathons in 51 days for [Sport Relief](/wiki/Sport_Relief "Sport Relief"), despite having no history of long-distance running. In 2016, she ran 27 marathons in 27 days in South Africa in honour of [Nelson Mandela](/wiki/Nelson_Mandela "Nelson Mandela"), raising £1.35 million. In addition to her native English, she has performed stand-up in Arabic, French, German, Russian and Spanish, and is an active supporter of [Europeanism](/wiki/Europeanism "Europeanism") and the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union").

A dedicated [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") activist, Izzard twice ran unsuccessfully for the party's [National Executive Committee](/wiki/National_Executive_Committee_of_the_Labour_Party "National Executive Committee of the Labour Party") and then joined as the most successful initially non-elected person after [Christine Shawcroft](/wiki/Christine_Shawcroft "Christine Shawcroft") resigned in March 2018. In 2022 and 2023, Izzard attempted to become the party's [prospective parliamentary candidate](/wiki/Prospective_parliamentary_candidate "Prospective parliamentary candidate") (for [Sheffield Central](/wiki/Sheffield_Central_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Sheffield Central (UK Parliament constituency)") and [Brighton Pavilion](/wiki/Brighton_Pavilion_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Brighton Pavilion (UK Parliament constituency)") respectively); she was not selected in the members' ballots.

## Early life and education

Izzard was born Edward John Izzard[[5]](#cite_note-chase2018-7) in [Aden](/wiki/Aden "Aden") (then in [Aden Colony](/wiki/Aden_Colony "Aden Colony") and now in [Yemen](/wiki/Yemen "Yemen"))[[6]](#cite_note-bono_intrvw-8) on 7 February 1962,[[7]](#cite_note-9) to English parents Dorothy Ella Izzard (1927–1968) and Harold John Michael Izzard (1928–2018). Their surname is of [French Huguenot](/wiki/Huguenot "Huguenot") origin.[[8]](#cite_note-10) Dorothy was a midwife and nurse, while Harold was an accountant who was working in Aden for [British Petroleum](/wiki/BP "BP") at the time of Edward's birth.[[9]](#cite_note-nytimes_james-11)[[10]](#cite_note-tlgrph_farndale-12) A brother, Mark, was born two years earlier.[[10]](#cite_note-tlgrph_farndale-12)

When Izzard was a year old, the family moved to Northern Ireland and settled in [Bangor](/wiki/Bangor,_County_Down "Bangor, County Down"), where they lived until Izzard was five.[[6]](#cite_note-bono_intrvw-8)[[9]](#cite_note-nytimes_james-11)[[11]](#cite_note-sydmornher_1-13)[[12]](#cite_note-indpndnt_sweeney-14) The family then moved to Wales, where they lived in [Skewen](/wiki/Skewen "Skewen").[[10]](#cite_note-tlgrph_farndale-12)

Izzard was six when her mother died of cancer.[[10]](#cite_note-tlgrph_farndale-12)[[11]](#cite_note-sydmornher_1-13)[[13]](#cite_note-mirror_neil-15) The siblings built a model railway to occupy their time while their mother was ill, which was later donated to [Bexhill Museum](/wiki/Bexhill_Museum "Bexhill Museum") in 2016.[[14]](#cite_note-16) Following the death, Izzard attended the [private](/wiki/Private_schools_in_the_United_Kingdom "Private schools in the United Kingdom") [St John's School](/wiki/St_Clare%27s_School,_Newton "St Clare's School, Newton") in [Newton](/wiki/Newton,_Porthcawl "Newton, Porthcawl"),[[15]](#cite_note-latimes_brownfield-17) [St Bede's Prep School](/wiki/Bede%27s_School,_Eastbourne "Bede's School, Eastbourne") in [Eastbourne](/wiki/Eastbourne "Eastbourne"),[[16]](#cite_note-18) and [Eastbourne College](/wiki/Eastbourne_College "Eastbourne College").[[10]](#cite_note-tlgrph_farndale-12)[[11]](#cite_note-sydmornher_1-13)[[17]](#cite_note-19) She has said that she knew she was [transgender](/wiki/Transgender "Transgender") at the age of four, after watching a boy being forced to wear a dress by his sisters,[[18]](#cite_note-nails-20) and knew she wanted to be an actor at the age of seven.[[19]](#cite_note-21)

She studied drama at the [University of Sheffield](/wiki/University_of_Sheffield "University of Sheffield").[[20]](#cite_note-22)

## Career

### Comedy

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/EddieIzzard.jpg/250px-EddieIzzard.jpg)](/wiki/File:EddieIzzard.jpg)

Izzard performing in December 2008

Izzard began to toy with comedy while at university with her friend Rob Ballard.[[21]](#cite_note-thsndytmes_appleyard-23)[[22]](#cite_note-latimes_taylor-24) The two took their act to the streets,[[21]](#cite_note-thsndytmes_appleyard-23)[[22]](#cite_note-latimes_taylor-24) often in the [Covent Garden](/wiki/Covent_Garden "Covent Garden") district of [London](/wiki/London "London").[[15]](#cite_note-latimes_brownfield-17)[[23]](#cite_note-indpndnt_burrell-25)[[24]](#cite_note-thisislondon_dessau-26) After splitting with Ballard, Izzard spent a great deal of the early 1980s working as a street performer in Europe and the United States. She says that she developed her comedic voice by talking to the audience while doing solo escape acts.[[25]](#cite_note-27) She then moved her act to the stand-up comedy venues of Britain, performing her routine for the first time at the Banana Cabaret in London's [Balham](/wiki/Balham "Balham") area.[[11]](#cite_note-sydmornher_1-13)[[26]](#cite_note-itunes_amstell-28)

In 1987, Izzard's first stage appearance was at the [Comedy Store](/wiki/The_Comedy_Store,_London "The Comedy Store, London") in London.[[12]](#cite_note-indpndnt_sweeney-14) She refined her comedy material throughout the 1980s and began earning recognition through improvisation in the early 1990s, in part at her own club, Raging Bull in [Soho](/wiki/Soho "Soho").[[24]](#cite_note-thisislondon_dessau-26) Her breakthrough came in 1991 after she performed her "raised by wolves" routine on the televised *Hysteria 3* [AIDS](/wiki/HIV/AIDS "HIV/AIDS") benefit.[[27]](#cite_note-29)

In 2000, for the comedy special *[Dress to Kill](/wiki/Dress_to_Kill_(Eddie_Izzard) "Dress to Kill (Eddie Izzard)")*, Izzard won two [Primetime Emmy Awards](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Awards "Primetime Emmy Awards"): [Outstanding Individual Performance in a Variety or Music Program](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Individual_Performance_in_a_Variety_or_Music_Program "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Individual Performance in a Variety or Music Program") and [Outstanding Writing for a Variety or Music Program](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Writing_for_a_Variety_Series "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Writing for a Variety Series"), while the special was nominated for [Outstanding Variety, Music, or Comedy Special](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Variety_Special_(Pre-Recorded) "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Variety Special (Pre-Recorded)").[[28]](#cite_note-30)

Izzard is fluent in French and has performed stand-up shows in the language; since 2014, she has also started to perform in Arabic, German, Russian and Spanish,[[29]](#cite_note-French-31) languages that she did not previously speak.[[30]](#cite_note-German-32)

### Acting

In 1994, Izzard's [West End drama](/wiki/West_End_theatre "West End theatre") debut as the lead in the world premiere of [David Mamet](/wiki/David_Mamet "David Mamet")'s *[The Cryptogram](/wiki/The_Cryptogram "The Cryptogram")* with [Lindsay Duncan](/wiki/Lindsay_Duncan "Lindsay Duncan"), in the production at London's Comedy Theatre. The success of that role led to a second starring role, in David Beaird's black comedy *900 Oneonta*. In 1995, she portrayed the title character in [Christopher Marlowe](/wiki/Christopher_Marlowe "Christopher Marlowe")'s *[Edward II](/wiki/Edward_II_(play) "Edward II (play)")*.[[31]](#cite_note-33)

In 1998, Izzard appeared briefly on stage with [Monty Python](/wiki/Monty_Python "Monty Python") in *The American Film Institute's Tribute to Monty Python* (also referred to as *[Monty Python Live at Aspen](/wiki/Monty_Python_Live_at_Aspen "Monty Python Live at Aspen")*). As part of an inside joke, she walked on stage with the five surviving Pythons and was summarily escorted off by [Eric Idle](/wiki/Eric_Idle "Eric Idle") and [Michael Palin](/wiki/Michael_Palin "Michael Palin") when attempting to participate in a discussion about how the group got together.[[32]](#cite_note-34) In July 2014, she appeared on stage with Monty Python during their live show *[Monty Python Live (Mostly)](/wiki/Monty_Python_Live_(Mostly) "Monty Python Live (Mostly)")* as the special guest in their "Blackmail" sketch.[[33]](#cite_note-35)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Eddie_Izzard_2013_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Eddie_Izzard_2013_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eddie_Izzard_2013_(cropped).jpg)

Izzard in 2013

Izzard portrayed comedian [Lenny Bruce](/wiki/Lenny_Bruce "Lenny Bruce") in the 1999 production of Julian Barry's 1971 play *Lenny*. In 2001, she replaced [Clive Owen](/wiki/Clive_Owen "Clive Owen") in [Peter Nichols](/wiki/Peter_Nichols_(playwright) "Peter Nichols (playwright)")' 1967 play *[A Day in the Death of Joe Egg](/wiki/A_Day_in_the_Death_of_Joe_Egg "A Day in the Death of Joe Egg")* at the [Comedy Theatre](/wiki/Comedy_Theatre "Comedy Theatre"). Izzard and [Victoria Hamilton](/wiki/Victoria_Hamilton "Victoria Hamilton") repeated their lead roles when the show was brought to Broadway in 2003 in the [Roundabout Theatre Company](/wiki/Roundabout_Theatre_Company "Roundabout Theatre Company") production. The revival received four [Tony Award](/wiki/Tony_Award "Tony Award") nominations, including Best Revival of a Play, Best Leading Actor and Best Leading Actress for its stars Izzard and Hamilton in their Broadway debuts, and Best Direction for Laurence Boswell. In June 2010, she replaced [James Spader](/wiki/James_Spader "James Spader") in the role of Jack Lawson in [David Mamet](/wiki/David_Mamet "David Mamet")'s play *[Race](/wiki/Race_(play) "Race (play)")* on Broadway.[[34]](#cite_note-nytimes_brantley-36)

Izzard has appeared in numerous films, starting with *[The Secret Agent](/wiki/The_Secret_Agent_(1996_film) "The Secret Agent (1996 film)")* in 1996, and has appeared as several real-life individuals, including [Charlie Chaplin](/wiki/Charlie_Chaplin "Charlie Chaplin") in *The Cat's Meow*, actor [Gustav von Wangenheim](/wiki/Gustav_von_Wangenheim "Gustav von Wangenheim") in *[Shadow of the Vampire](/wiki/Shadow_of_the_Vampire "Shadow of the Vampire"),* General [Erich Fellgiebel](/wiki/Erich_Fellgiebel "Erich Fellgiebel") in *[Valkyrie](/wiki/Valkyrie_(film) "Valkyrie (film)")* and wartime pioneer of [radar](/wiki/Radar "Radar") [Robert Watson-Watt](/wiki/Robert_Watson-Watt "Robert Watson-Watt") in the BBC drama film *[Castles in the Sky](/wiki/Castles_in_the_Sky_(film) "Castles in the Sky (film)")*. Other roles have included Mr Kite in *[Across the Universe](/wiki/Across_the_Universe_(film) "Across the Universe (film)")*, Lussurioso in *[Revengers Tragedy](/wiki/Revengers_Tragedy "Revengers Tragedy")* and criminal expert [Roman Nagel](/wiki/List_of_characters_from_the_Ocean%27s_Trilogy#Roman_Nagel "List of characters from the Ocean's Trilogy") in *Ocean's Twelve* and *Ocean's Thirteen*. Her voice work has included the titular "It" in *[Five Children and It](/wiki/Five_Children_and_It_(2004_film) "Five Children and It (2004 film)")*, Nigel in *The Wild* and the mouse warrior [Reepicheep](/wiki/Reepicheep "Reepicheep") in *[The Chronicles of Narnia: Prince Caspian](/wiki/The_Chronicles_of_Narnia:_Prince_Caspian "The Chronicles of Narnia: Prince Caspian")*. Izzard declined to reprise the role as Reepicheep, a role understudied by [Simon Pegg](/wiki/Simon_Pegg "Simon Pegg") in *[The Chronicles of Narnia: The Voyage of the Dawn Treader](/wiki/The_Chronicles_of_Narnia:_The_Voyage_of_the_Dawn_Treader "The Chronicles of Narnia: The Voyage of the Dawn Treader")*. Izzard has stated that she felt she learned to act while working on the film *[Circus](/wiki/Circus_(2000_film) "Circus (2000 film)")*.[[35]](#cite_note-37)

In 2009, Izzard was the subject of [Sarah Townsend](/wiki/Sarah_Townsend "Sarah Townsend")'s documentary *Believe: The Eddie Izzard Story*,[[36]](#cite_note-38) which addresses [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC")'s [Watchdog](/wiki/Watchdog_(TV_programme) "Watchdog (TV programme)")[[37]](#cite_note-39) reporting[[38]](#cite_note-Double-2013-Stand-Up-40) of "recycling material from an old tour".[[39]](#cite_note-independent.co.uk-2164715-41)[[40]](#cite_note-independent.co.uk-2162947-42)[[41]](#cite_note-auntiemomo-43)

Izzard appeared in the 2009 BBC science fiction miniseries *[The Day of the Triffids](/wiki/The_Day_of_the_Triffids_(2009_TV_series) "The Day of the Triffids (2009 TV series)")*, based on the [1951 novel](/wiki/The_Day_of_the_Triffids "The Day of the Triffids"), alongside [Jason Priestley](/wiki/Jason_Priestley "Jason Priestley"), [Vanessa Redgrave](/wiki/Vanessa_Redgrave "Vanessa Redgrave"), [Joely Richardson](/wiki/Joely_Richardson "Joely Richardson"), [Dougray Scott](/wiki/Dougray_Scott "Dougray Scott") and [Brian Cox](/wiki/Brian_Cox_(actor) "Brian Cox (actor)").[[42]](#cite_note-telegraph_1-44) She played Dr. Hatteras, a sceptical psychology professor, in the Showtime series *[United States of Tara](/wiki/United_States_of_Tara "United States of Tara")*[[43]](#cite_note-hitfix_fienberg-45) and appeared in six episodes of the 2013–15 American psychological horror television series *[Hannibal](/wiki/Hannibal_(TV_series) "Hannibal (TV series)")* as Dr. Abel Gideon.[[44]](#cite_note-46) In 2021, she appeared in the television series *[The Lost Symbol](/wiki/The_Lost_Symbol_(TV_series) "The Lost Symbol (TV series)")* based on [Dan Brown](/wiki/Dan_Brown "Dan Brown")'s 2009 [novel of the same name](/wiki/The_Lost_Symbol "The Lost Symbol").[[45]](#cite_note-47)

At the London [2012 Paralympic Games](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics"), Izzard presented the medals to the athletes who had won the 800m [T54](/wiki/T54_(classification) "T54 (classification)") race, including gold medalist [David Weir](/wiki/David_Weir_(wheelchair_athlete) "David Weir (wheelchair athlete)").[[46]](#cite_note-48)

She has appeared on a number of episodes of [BBC One](/wiki/BBC_One "BBC One")'s *[Have I Got News for You](/wiki/Have_I_Got_News_for_You "Have I Got News for You")* and as a guest on *[The Daily Show](/wiki/The_Daily_Show "The Daily Show")*.[[47]](#cite_note-49) In 2017, she read excerpts from her autobiography *Believe Me* for [BBC Radio 4](/wiki/BBC_Radio_4 "BBC Radio 4")'s *[Book of the Week](/wiki/Book_of_the_Week "Book of the Week")*.[[48]](#cite_note-50)

From 25 January to 3 March 2024, Izzard performed a one-person version of Shakespeare's *[Hamlet](/wiki/Hamlet "Hamlet")*, adapted by her brother Mark and directed by [Selina Cadell](/wiki/Selina_Cadell "Selina Cadell"), at the [Greenwich House Theater](/wiki/Greenwich_House "Greenwich House") in New York.[[49]](#cite_note-51) The run was extended three times. The show transferred to [Riverside Studios](/wiki/Riverside_Studios "Riverside Studios"), London, with previews from 23 May 2024.[[50]](#cite_note-52)[[51]](#cite_note-53)

## Charity work

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Eddie_Izzard%27s_flag_for_Northern_Ireland.svg/250px-Eddie_Izzard%27s_flag_for_Northern_Ireland.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Eddie_Izzard%27s_flag_for_Northern_Ireland.svg)

Flag used by Izzard to represent Northern Ireland during the 2009 Sport Relief marathon. The dark green background references the Northern Ireland football jersey, while the white dove symbolises peace.[[52]](#cite_note-54)[[53]](#cite_note-55)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Eddie_Izzard%27s_flag_for_the_Home_Nations.svg/250px-Eddie_Izzard%27s_flag_for_the_Home_Nations.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Eddie_Izzard%27s_flag_for_the_Home_Nations.svg)

Flag carried by Izzard during the Sport Relief Mile event in 2010.[[54]](#cite_note-56)

On 27 July 2009, with only five weeks' training and no significant prior running experience, Izzard began seven weeks of back-to-back marathon runs (with Sundays off) across the UK to raise money for [Sport Relief](/wiki/Sport_Relief "Sport Relief").[[55]](#cite_note-bbc_heald-57) She ran from London to [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff") to [Belfast](/wiki/Belfast "Belfast") to [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh") and back to London, carrying the flag of the country—England, Scotland, or Wales—in which she was running. In Northern Ireland, she carried a self-designed green flag bearing a white dove. The blog *Eddie Iz Running* documented the 43 marathons in 51 days, covering at least 27 miles per day (totalling more than 1,100 miles), ending on 15 September 2009. The BBC reported that Izzard took 5 to 10 hours per marathon.[[56]](#cite_note-58)[[57]](#cite_note-comicrelief_1-59) Izzard received a special award at [BBC Sports Personality of the Year](/wiki/BBC_Sports_Personality_of_the_Year "BBC Sports Personality of the Year") in 2009 for these achievements.[[58]](#cite_note-bbc_20081213-60) In March 2010, she took part in the Sport Relief Mile event.[[59]](#cite_note-telegraph_nikkhah-61)

On 16 February 2016, the BBC announced that Izzard would attempt to run 27 marathons in 27 days through South Africa for Sport Relief.[[60]](#cite_note-62) The significance of the number 27 came from the number of years [Nelson Mandela](/wiki/Nelson_Mandela "Nelson Mandela") was held in prison. In total, she would aim to run more than 700 miles in temperatures of up to 40 °C. Izzard had attempted such a project in South Africa in 2012, but withdrew due to health concerns.[[61]](#cite_note-63) She completed the first marathon on 23 February 2016, completing the marathon challenge on 20 March 2016 at the statue of Mandela in front of the [Union Buildings](/wiki/Union_Buildings "Union Buildings") in [Pretoria](/wiki/Pretoria "Pretoria"). Because she had spent a day in hospital, she had to run two consecutive marathons on this last day. She raised more than £1.35M for Sport Relief.[[62]](#cite_note-64) A BBC documentary detailing the feat was broadcast on 28 March.[[63]](#cite_note-65)

On 8 December 2020, Izzard announced[[64]](#cite_note-Chortle-66) that she would attempt to run 31 marathons and perform 31 stand-up gigs, in the 31 days of January 2021 to raise money for a range of charities including Fareshare, [Walking With The Wounded](/wiki/Walking_With_The_Wounded "Walking With The Wounded"), [Care International](/wiki/Care_International "Care International"), United to Combat Neglected Tropical Diseases and [Covenant House](/wiki/Covenant_House "Covenant House").[[65]](#cite_note-Metro_-_legs_knackered-67) The series of marathons raised in excess of £275,000.[[66]](#cite_note-68)

## Activism

### Political views

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Eddie_Izzard_with_EU_and_UK_flags.jpg/250px-Eddie_Izzard_with_EU_and_UK_flags.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eddie_Izzard_with_EU_and_UK_flags.jpg)

Izzard with the flags of the UK and European Union in 2006

Izzard is a vocal supporter of [Europeanism](/wiki/European_values "European values") and [European integration](/wiki/European_integration "European integration"), and has campaigned in support of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"). In May 2005, she appeared on the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC")'s political debate show *[Question Time](/wiki/Question_Time_(TV_programme) "Question Time (TV programme)")*, describing herself as a "British-European", comparing this with other cultural identities such as "African-American". As part of her campaigning, Izzard was one of the first people to spend a euro in London. This pan-European approach has influenced her work, regularly performing in French[[23]](#cite_note-indpndnt_burrell-25)[[43]](#cite_note-hitfix_fienberg-45) and occasionally in German.[[24]](#cite_note-thisislondon_dessau-26) On a June 2017 episode of *[Real Time with Bill Maher](/wiki/Real_Time_with_Bill_Maher "Real Time with Bill Maher")*, she claimed to be working in English, French, German and Spanish.[[30]](#cite_note-German-32)[[29]](#cite_note-French-31)

Izzard campaigned in favour of replacing [first-past-the-post](/wiki/First-past-the-post "First-past-the-post") with the [alternative vote](/wiki/Instant-runoff_voting "Instant-runoff voting") as a system for electing [MPs](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") in [a 2011 referendum](/wiki/2011_United_Kingdom_Alternative_Vote_referendum "2011 United Kingdom Alternative Vote referendum")[[67]](#cite_note-69)[[68]](#cite_note-70) and is a supporter of the [Labour Campaign for Electoral Reform](/wiki/Labour_Campaign_for_Electoral_Reform "Labour Campaign for Electoral Reform"). She is also a proponent of [British republicanism](/wiki/Republicanism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Republicanism in the United Kingdom"), believing that the UK should have a democratically elected head of state instead of a monarchy.[[69]](#cite_note-71) She has stated that she is a [social democrat](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy"), but not a [socialist](/wiki/Socialism "Socialism").[[70]](#cite_note-72) During the [2014 Scottish independence referendum](/wiki/2014_Scottish_independence_referendum "2014 Scottish independence referendum"), Izzard led a campaign encouraging Scottish people not to vote for independence and said the rest of the UK would feel a "deep sense of loss" if Scotland were to leave.[[71]](#cite_note-73)

Izzard campaigned unsuccessfully against the closure of the departments of Drama and Languages, Linguistics and Translation at the [University of East Anglia](/wiki/University_of_East_Anglia "University of East Anglia"), although the department of Drama was later reprieved.[[72]](#cite_note-UEA-74)

### Labour Party

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Eddie_Izzard_and_Tony_Blair.jpg/250px-Eddie_Izzard_and_Tony_Blair.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eddie_Izzard_and_Tony_Blair.jpg)

Izzard (left) travels to [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") with Labour Prime Minister [Tony Blair](/wiki/Tony_Blair "Tony Blair") in 2006

Izzard joined the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") in 1995[[73]](#cite_note-:0-75) and in 1998 was listed as one of the largest private donors to the party.[[74]](#cite_note-bbc_19980830-76) In 2008, she donated nearly £10,000 to it.[[75]](#cite_note-politics_1-77) She appeared in [party political broadcasts](/wiki/Party_political_broadcast "Party political broadcast") for the Labour Party in the run-up to the [2005 general election](/wiki/2005_United_Kingdom_general_election "2005 United Kingdom general election") and [2009 European election](/wiki/2009_European_Parliament_election_in_the_United_Kingdom "2009 European Parliament election in the United Kingdom"), as well as a 2010 election video entitled *Brilliant Britain*. During the [2015 general election](/wiki/2015_United_Kingdom_general_election "2015 United Kingdom general election"), she attended a rally with fellow comedian [Ben Elton](/wiki/Ben_Elton "Ben Elton") and actor [Sally Lindsay](/wiki/Sally_Lindsay "Sally Lindsay").[[76]](#cite_note-78) Expressing support for Labour in the [2017 general election](/wiki/2017_United_Kingdom_general_election "2017 United Kingdom general election"), she said that the then-leader of the party [Jeremy Corbyn](/wiki/Jeremy_Corbyn "Jeremy Corbyn") "believes in what he says".[[77]](#cite_note-independent-79)

At various times, Izzard said she would run for [Mayor of London](/wiki/Mayor_of_London "Mayor of London") in 2020.[[78]](#cite_note-80)[[79]](#cite_note-81) When asked on the comedy panel show *[The Last Leg](/wiki/The_Last_Leg "The Last Leg")* why she would be elected, Izzard replied, "[Boris Johnson](/wiki/Boris_Johnson "Boris Johnson")."[[80]](#cite_note-82) However, she was not a candidate for the delayed [2021 London mayoral election](/wiki/2021_London_mayoral_election "2021 London mayoral election").[[81]](#cite_note-83) Izzard unsuccessfully ran for a seat on the [National Executive Committee of the Labour Party](/wiki/National_Executive_Committee_of_the_Labour_Party "National Executive Committee of the Labour Party") in 2016 and 2018.[[82]](#cite_note-84)[[83]](#cite_note-85)[[84]](#cite_note-86) After [Christine Shawcroft](/wiki/Christine_Shawcroft "Christine Shawcroft") resigned in March 2018, Izzard replaced her as the next runner-up, but failed to secure re-election that summer.[[85]](#cite_note-87)[[86]](#cite_note-88)

On 25 August 2022, Izzard stated in an interview on [BBC Radio Sheffield](/wiki/BBC_Radio_Sheffield "BBC Radio Sheffield") that she would like to ask the local [Constituency Labour Party](/wiki/Constituency_Labour_Party "Constituency Labour Party") to stand in [Sheffield Central](/wiki/Sheffield_Central_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Sheffield Central (UK Parliament constituency)"), replacing the incumbent MP [Paul Blomfield](/wiki/Paul_Blomfield "Paul Blomfield"), who is standing down at the [2024 election](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "2024 United Kingdom general election").[[87]](#cite_note-89) Local reaction, according to the *[Sheffield Star](/wiki/Sheffield_Star "Sheffield Star")* newspaper, was mixed.[[88]](#cite_note-90) She launched her election campaign on 11 October.[[89]](#cite_note-91) On 5 December, it was reported that a local [Sheffield City](/wiki/Sheffield_City_Council "Sheffield City Council") councillor had been selected as the party's candidate for the [safe seat](/wiki/Safe_seat "Safe seat"), with Izzard coming second in the members' vote.[[73]](#cite_note-:0-75)

In August 2023, Izzard announced her campaign to become the Labour candidate for [Brighton Pavilion](/wiki/Brighton_Pavilion_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Brighton Pavilion (UK Parliament constituency)") at the next general election, following the decision of the incumbent [Green](/wiki/Green_Party_of_England_and_Wales "Green Party of England and Wales") MP [Caroline Lucas](/wiki/Caroline_Lucas "Caroline Lucas") not to recontest the seat.[[90]](#cite_note-92) On 17 December 2023, it was announced that Izzard had not been selected as a Labour candidate at the next general election and that [Tom Gray](/wiki/Tom_Gray_(activist) "Tom Gray (activist)") would be contesting Brighton Pavilion on behalf of the Labour Party instead.[[91]](#cite_note-93)

## Comedic style

Izzard uses a [stream-of-consciousness](/wiki/Stream_of_consciousness "Stream of consciousness") delivery that jumps between topics, saying in a 2004 interview with *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* that "it's the [oral tradition](/wiki/Oral_tradition "Oral tradition") [...] human beings have been doing it for thousands of years".[[92]](#cite_note-guardian_bedell-94) Her bent towards the surreal went so far as to produce a sitcom called *[Cows](/wiki/Cows_(TV_series) "Cows (TV series)")* in 1997 for [Channel 4](/wiki/Channel_4 "Channel 4"), a live-action comedy with actors dressed in cow suits.[[93]](#cite_note-auntimomo_1-95) She has cited [Monty Python](/wiki/Monty_Python "Monty Python") as her biggest influence, and Python member [John Cleese](/wiki/John_Cleese "John Cleese") once referred to her as "the lost Python".[[12]](#cite_note-indpndnt_sweeney-14)

## Personal life

Izzard has identified as [genderfluid](/wiki/Genderfluid "Genderfluid").[[94]](#cite_note-them-96)[[95]](#cite_note-97) She has stated that she realised by age four that she was a transvestite, but did not come out until the age of 29.[[96]](#cite_note-tough-98) According to her 2017 memoir *Believe Me*, she first cross-dressed in public at the age of 23 with the help of a lesbian friend, an experience which ended in a verbal confrontation with three 13-year-old girls who followed Izzard home from a public toilet.[[97]](#cite_note-99) She started to publicly identify as transvestite in venues such as the Edinburgh Festival as early as 1992.[[98]](#cite_note-observer_05_2001-100)[[99]](#cite_note-independent_lister-101) She remarked in *[Unrepeatable](/wiki/Unrepeatable "Unrepeatable")* (1994) "Women wear what they want and so do I." In 2004 Izzard said "I'm a straight transvestite or male lesbian […] I'd say I'm a complete boy plus half a girl."[[96]](#cite_note-tough-98) She stated in 2014 that the way she dressed was neither part of her performance, nor a [sexual fetish](/wiki/Sexual_fetish "Sexual fetish"): "I don't call it [drag](/wiki/Drag_(clothing) "Drag (clothing)"); I don't even call it cross-dressing. It's just wearing a dress. It's not about artifice. It's about me just expressing myself."[[100]](#cite_note-102)

In 2015 she expressed the conviction that being transgender is caused by genetics and that someday this will be scientifically proven; in preparation, she has had her own [genome](/wiki/Genome "Genome") sequenced.[[101]](#cite_note-103) In 2016 Izzard described herself as "somewhat boy-ish and somewhat girl-ish"[[18]](#cite_note-nails-20) and as a "[transgender](/wiki/Transgender "Transgender") guy".[[102]](#cite_note-104)

When asked in 2019 what pronouns she preferred, Izzard responded, "either 'he' or 'she'" and explained, "If I am in boy mode, then 'he', or girl mode, 'she'".[[103]](#cite_note-windy-105) In 2020, she requested *she/her* pronouns for an appearance on the TV show *Portrait Artist of the Year* and said she wants "to be based in girl mode from now on".[[104]](#cite_note-SkyDec-106) In March 2023, she announced that she would begin using the name Suzy in addition to Eddie, saying that she is "going to be Suzy Eddie Izzard".[[2]](#cite_note-SuzyHuffPost-2)[[1]](#cite_note-SuzyIndependent-1) Explaining that she had wanted to use the name Suzy since she was 10 years old, she added that people "can choose" which name they want to use to refer to her,[[1]](#cite_note-SuzyIndependent-1)[[2]](#cite_note-SuzyHuffPost-2) and that she would keep using Eddie Izzard as her public name since it is more widely recognised.[[105]](#cite_note-107)

Izzard keeps her romantic life private, citing the wishes of her companions not wanting to become content for her show.[[106]](#cite_note-Hollywood_Commons-108) She once dated Irish singer [Sarah Townsend](/wiki/Sarah_Townsend "Sarah Townsend"), whom Izzard first met while running a venue at the [Edinburgh Festival Fringe](/wiki/Edinburgh_Festival_Fringe "Edinburgh Festival Fringe") in 1989.[[107]](#cite_note-newsscotsman_hoggard-109) Townsend later created the documentary *Believe: The Eddie Izzard Story*.[[23]](#cite_note-indpndnt_burrell-25)

Izzard is an [atheist](/wiki/Atheism "Atheism"). During the 2008 *Stripped* tour, she said, "I was warming the material up in New York, where one night, literally on stage, I realised I didn't believe in God at all. I just didn't think there was anyone upstairs."[[106]](#cite_note-Hollywood_Commons-108) She has since described herself as a [spiritual atheist](/wiki/Spiritual_but_not_religious "Spiritual but not religious"), saying, "I don't believe in the guy upstairs, I believe in us."[[108]](#cite_note-110)

Izzard supports [Crystal Palace](/wiki/Crystal_Palace_F.C. "Crystal Palace F.C.") and became an associate director at the club on 16 July 2012.[[109]](#cite_note-111) She is also a train modeller.[[110]](#cite_note-112)

## Honours

In 2003, Izzard received an honorary [Doctorate of Letters](/wiki/Doctorate_of_Letters "Doctorate of Letters") from the [University of East Anglia](/wiki/University_of_East_Anglia "University of East Anglia"), [Norwich](/wiki/Norwich "Norwich"), for her work promoting "modern languages and tolerance of other cultures and lifestyles", and for having "transcended national barriers" with humour.[[72]](#cite_note-UEA-74)[[111]](#cite_note-113) She has also received honorary doctorates from the [University of Sunderland](/wiki/University_of_Sunderland "University of Sunderland") in 2012,[[112]](#cite_note-114) [York St John University](/wiki/York_St_John_University "York St John University") in 2018,[[113]](#cite_note-115) the [University of Sussex](/wiki/University_of_Sussex "University of Sussex") in 2025[[114]](#cite_note-116) and the [University of Sheffield](/wiki/University_of_Sheffield "University of Sheffield") in 2006,[[115]](#cite_note-shef_honrdoct-117) where she had spent a year on an Accounting and Financial Management course in the early 1980s and established the now-defunct Alternative Productions Society in the Union of Students with the aim of promoting [fringe-based](/wiki/Fringe_theatre "Fringe theatre") arts. She was elected Honorary President of Sheffield's [Students' Union](/wiki/University_of_Sheffield_Union_of_Students "University of Sheffield Union of Students") in 2010.[[116]](#cite_note-shef_elec2010-118)

Izzard's website won the [Yahoo](/wiki/Yahoo "Yahoo") People's Choice Award in 2004 and a [Webby Award](/wiki/Webby_Award "Webby Award") in 2005.[[117]](#cite_note-119)[[118]](#cite_note-120)

In 2007, Izzard was listed as number 3 of the 100 Greatest British National Comedians (behind [Peter Kay](/wiki/Peter_Kay "Peter Kay") at number 2 and [Billy Connolly](/wiki/Billy_Connolly "Billy Connolly") at number 1) as part of British television station Channel 4's ongoing *100 Greatest ...* series, and was ranked 5th in 2010.[[119]](#cite_note-channel4_1-121)

In 2013, Izzard received the 6th Annual Outstanding Lifetime Achievement Award in Cultural Humanism,[[120]](#cite_note-122)[[121]](#cite_note-123) which is presented at Harvard University each year by the Humanist Community at Harvard,[[122]](#cite_note-124) the American Humanist Association and the Harvard Community of Humanists, Atheists, and Agnostics.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2015, Izzard was chosen by readers of *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* as their 2014 public language champion. The award was announced at the Guardian and British Academy 2014 Schools Language Awards as part of the annual Language Festival.[[123]](#cite_note-125)
